examples: Text mining over 50,000 support tickets revealed that 30% of escalations mentioned the same three product features.
We use text mining to extract structured fields — dates, amounts, parties — from unstructured legal documents.

humor: A model that routes each input to specialized sub-networks. Like a hospital with specialists instead of one doctor who does everything. Except the routing nurse sometimes sends you to cardiology for a broken toe.
examples: The mixture-of-experts architecture activates only 2 of 8 expert layers per token, reducing inference compute by 4x.
Llama 4 Maverick uses a mixture-of-experts design, which is why it has 400B total parameters but only uses a fraction per forward pass.

humor: A model where most parameters are inactive for any given input. Has billions of parameters but only uses a fraction at a time. The AI equivalent of a gym membership.
examples: The sparse model has 120B parameters but only activates 30B per token, giving it strong performance with manageable inference cost.
We chose a sparse model over a dense equivalent because it fit within our GPU memory budget while maintaining comparable accuracy.

humor: Decides which expert handles each input in a mixture-of-experts model. The unsung traffic cop of modern architectures. When it works, nobody notices. When it fails, everyone does.
examples: The routing network assigns each token to the top 2 experts based on a learned gating function.
A poorly trained routing network leads to expert collapse — all tokens get sent to the same expert, wasting the other seven.

humor: Distributing work evenly across systems. A solved problem in web servers. An active research problem in mixture-of-experts models. Context matters.
examples: We added a load-balancing loss to the MoE training objective to ensure all experts receive roughly equal traffic.
Load balancing across our inference cluster ensures no single GPU becomes a bottleneck during peak request volume.

humor: Using a small model to draft tokens and a large model to verify them. The AI equivalent of having an intern write the first draft and a senior partner approve it.
examples: Speculative decoding doubled our inference throughput by letting a 1B model draft 5 tokens at a time, with the 70B model accepting or rejecting each one.
We implemented speculative decoding on our self-hosted model and cut average latency from 800ms to 350ms per response.

humor: The specific mechanism that sends tokens to the right expert. Getting this wrong means your language model routes a French sentence to the math expert. It does not go well.
examples: MoE routing uses a softmax gating function to compute a probability distribution over experts for each token.
We monitored the routing patterns and found that one expert was handling 60% of all tokens — a sign that the routing had collapsed.

humor: Chaining AI agents to complete multi-step tasks. The flowchart looks beautiful. The error log looks like a novel.
examples: Our agentic workflow chains a research agent, a drafting agent, and a review agent to produce compliance reports end-to-end.
The agentic workflow handles 80% of cases automatically and escalates the remaining 20% to a human reviewer with full context.

humor: Teaching machines to see. Took decades of research to reach a point where your phone can identify a dog breed instantly but still cannot tell if that is your finger over the lens.
examples: Computer vision powers the quality inspection system on the factory floor, flagging defective parts at 120 units per minute.
We added computer vision to the document processing pipeline to extract tables and figures that text-only models miss.

humor: When a model trained on AI-generated data degrades over generations. The ouroboros of machine learning — the snake eating its own tail until there is nothing left but noise.
examples: We filter AI-generated content from our training data to prevent model collapse in future fine-tuning iterations.
Model collapse was visible after three generations of self-training — the outputs became repetitive and lost diversity.

humor: An open-source workflow automation tool. Pronounced 'n-eight-n.' The name looks like a typo that shipped to production and everyone was too polite to correct.
examples: We use n8n to orchestrate the data pipeline that feeds our RAG system — it pulls from Slack, Notion, and Confluence on a schedule.
The n8n workflow triggers a model inference on every new support ticket and auto-tags it before a human sees it.

humor: A point release that exists because version numbers in AI have become a competitive sport. The delta from 5.2 to 5.4 is real but not as dramatic as the announcement implies.
examples: GPT-5.4 improved structured output reliability, which reduced our JSON parsing error rate from 3% to under 0.5%.
We upgraded from GPT-5.2 to GPT-5.4 for the function calling improvements — the accuracy gain was modest but consistent.

humor: Converting text to a standard form before processing. The preprocessing step everyone skips and then wonders why 'NYC' and 'New York City' are not matching.
examples: Text normalization lowercases all input, expands abbreviations, and strips stray unicode before the text hits the embedding model.
Skipping text normalization caused duplicate entries in the knowledge base — 'U.S.A.' and 'USA' were indexed as separate entities.

humor: Removing common words like 'the' and 'is' from text. The only NLP technique where you improve results by literally throwing data away.
examples: Stop word removal cut the BM25 index size by 30% and improved query precision on our technical documentation search.
We kept 'not' in the stop word exception list after the system started returning results for 'working' when users searched 'not working.'

humor: Chopping suffixes off words to find the root. Fast, brutal, and produces outputs like 'studi' from 'studies.' The chainsaw of text preprocessing — effective but not pretty.
examples: Porter stemming maps 'running', 'runs', and 'runner' to the same stem, improving recall in the keyword search index.
Stemming caused a false match between 'universal' and 'university' — we switched to lemmatization for the search pipeline.

humor: Reducing words to their dictionary form, properly. Stemming's more educated sibling who actually checks the dictionary before answering.
examples: Lemmatization correctly maps 'better' to 'good' and 'went' to 'go', which stemming cannot do.
We use spaCy's lemmatizer in the preprocessing pipeline so that different inflections of the same word match during retrieval.

humor: Labeling every word with its grammatical role. The model knows it is a noun. The model does not know why you care. You need it for the next six pipeline stages.
examples: POS tagging lets the lemmatizer distinguish between 'saw' as a verb (→ see) and 'saw' as a noun (→ saw).
We use POS tags to filter out verbs and adjectives when building the noun-phrase index for entity extraction.

humor: Mapping grammatical relationships between words in a sentence. The visualization looks like a subway map. Understanding it requires a linguistics degree or three hours on Wikipedia.
examples: Dependency parsing reveals that in 'Apple acquired Beats,' 'Apple' is the subject and 'Beats' is the object of the acquisition.
We use dependency parse trees to extract subject-verb-object triples for populating the knowledge graph from unstructured text.

humor: Figuring out that 'she,' 'the CEO,' and 'Dr. Park' all refer to the same person. Easy for humans. A research paper for machines.
examples: Coreference resolution links 'the company' in paragraph 3 back to 'Acme Corp' in paragraph 1, enabling cross-paragraph entity tracking.
Without coreference resolution, the summarizer attributed actions to 'they' without ever specifying who 'they' referred to.

humor: Turning words into vectors so 'king minus man plus woman equals queen.' The most cited example in every NLP intro course, and the reason students think all of NLP is this elegant. It is not.
examples: Word embeddings from Word2Vec gave the search system a basic notion of synonymy — 'car' and 'automobile' now return overlapping results.
We replaced static word embeddings with contextual embeddings from BERT when polysemy was causing retrieval errors.

humor: Determining if text is positive, negative, or neutral. Works great on 'I love this product.' Falls apart on 'Oh great, another update that breaks everything.'
examples: Sentiment analysis on customer reviews feeds into the dashboard that product managers check every morning.
We run sentiment analysis on support tickets at intake — negative sentiment tickets get higher priority in the queue.

humor: Assigning labels to text. One of the oldest NLP tasks and still the one that pays the bills while the flashier tasks get the conference talks.
examples: The text classification model routes incoming emails to one of 12 department queues with 93% accuracy.
We fine-tuned a text classification model on 5,000 labeled examples and deployed it behind a REST API for real-time categorization.

humor: Breaking words into pieces when the whole word is too rare. The reason the model knows 'unhappiness' is related to 'happy' but tokenizes your company name into meaningless syllables.
examples: Subword tokenization splits 'unhappiness' into ['un', 'happiness'], letting the model leverage morphological structure.
Our domain-specific terms were being split into 5-6 subword tokens — we trained a custom tokenizer with domain vocabulary to fix it.

humor: Predicting the next token given previous tokens. The foundational task behind every LLM. Deceptively simple to describe. Deceptively expensive to do well.
examples: Language modeling on our internal codebase taught the model our naming conventions, API patterns, and architectural style.
The perplexity of the language model on held-out test data tells us how well it has learned the statistical patterns of our domain text.

humor: A step-by-step procedure for solving a problem. In job interviews, it means inverting a binary tree. In production, it means a for loop with an if statement.
examples: The recommendation algorithm ranks products by a weighted combination of collaborative filtering scores and purchase recency.
We replaced the hand-tuned sorting algorithm with a learned ranking model once the number of signals exceeded what a human could reason about.

humor: IBM's marketing term for AI before everyone agreed to just call it AI. Watson won Jeopardy! in 2011 and the term has been coasting on that momentum ever since.
examples: The enterprise sales deck still says cognitive computing because the procurement team approved that term in 2018 and nobody wants to redo the paperwork.
We migrated from the legacy cognitive computing platform to a modern RAG pipeline and halved both latency and cost.

humor: An algorithm that wanders randomly through probability space until it accidentally finds the right answer. The fact that this is mathematically rigorous is either reassuring or deeply unsettling.
examples: We used MCMC sampling to estimate the posterior distribution over model hyperparameters when grid search was computationally infeasible.
The MCMC chain needed 500,000 iterations to converge on the 12-dimensional parameter space — we let it run overnight and checked the trace plots in the morning.

humor: When a model does something nobody trained it to do. Sometimes that thing is useful. Sometimes it is finding and decrypting your benchmark answer key. Surprise.
examples: The model developed an emergent ability to solve simple algebra problems despite never being explicitly trained on math — it just appeared at the 70B parameter scale.
We discovered emergent behavior in our agent when it started combining tools in sequences we never anticipated, some of which were actually more efficient than our hand-designed chains.

humor: The safety constraints everyone agrees are important and nobody wants to be the one to implement. Usually added after the first time the chatbot says something that ends up on Twitter.
examples: The guardrails layer validates every model response against our content policy before returning it to the user, adding 50ms of latency that nobody complains about.
We added output guardrails after the model recommended a competitor's product to a customer — the rule now filters any response mentioning brands outside our approved list.
